file_name: NETGEAR,INC_04_21_2003-EX-10.16-AMENDMENT TO THE DISTRIBUTOR AGREEMENT BETWEEN INGRAM MICRO AND NETGEAR.txt

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement, including its attachment and order acknowledgments under the Agreement, constitutes the entire agreement between Distributor and NETGEAR with respect to the purchase, resale and distribution of the Products and is governed by the laws of the State of California except that body of law dealing with conflicts of law.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement, including its attachment and order acknowledgments under the Agreement, constitutes the entire agreement between Distributor and NETGEAR with respect to the purchase, resale and distribution of the Products and is governed by the laws of a jurisdiction chosen by NETGEAR.
perturbation.explanation: This change allows NETGEAR to arbitrarily choose the governing law, which contradicts established principles of contract law and could violate due process if the chosen jurisdiction has no connection to the contract.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code Section 1646 states that a contract is to be interpreted according to the law and usage of the place where it is to be performed, or, if that is not indicated, according to the law and usage of the place where it is made. Giving NETGEAR unilateral power to choose the governing law goes against this principle.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 1646 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> Code Search >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 3. OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 2. CONTRACTS [1549 - 1701] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 3. INTERPRETATION OF CONTRACTS [1635 - 1663] ( Title 3 enacted 1872. ) 1646. A contract is to be interpreted according to the law and usage of the place where it is to be performed; or, if it does not indicate a place of performance, according to the law and usage of the place where it is made. (Enacted 1872.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text allows NETGEAR to choose the governing law, which contradicts California Civil Code Section 1646, as provided in Scraped Law Snippet 1. The original text specified California law, but the changed text provides NETGEAR with unilateral power to select the jurisdiction. This is a contradiction because Section 1646 states that a contract should be interpreted according to the law of the place of performance or where it is made. By allowing NETGEAR to arbitrarily choose the governing law, the modified text undermines this principle and creates a legal contradiction as the chosen jurisdiction may have no connection to the contract or its performance, making it unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: For the purposes of this provision, distributor shall mean a company acquiring products directly from NETGEAR for resale or license to dealers or other second tier resellers which in turn resell or license the products to end use customers.
perturbation.changed_text: For the purposes of this provision, distributor shall mean any entity acquiring products from NETGEAR, regardless of whether for resale or its own internal use. The term 'end use customers' is defined as anyone in possession of the product.
perturbation.explanation: By including entities acquiring for internal use as distributors and redefining 'end use customers' this broadens the definition and could violate distribution agreements by not distinguishing between resellers and end consumers, impacting sales tax and warranty obligations.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Revenue and Taxation Code Section 6006
perturbation.law_explanation: California Revenue and Taxation Code Section 6006 defines 'sale' to include the transfer of title or possession of tangible personal property for a consideration.  Treating internal use as distribution obscures the point of sale, making tax collection and remittance unclear, which the law requires.

General Provisions and Definitions [6001 - 6024] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 36. ) 6006. “Sale” means and includes: (a) Any transfer of title or possession, exchange, or barter, conditional or otherwise, in any manner or by any means whatsoever, of tangible personal property for a consideration. “Transfer of possession” includes only transactions found by the board to be in lieu of a transfer of title, exchange, or barter. (b) The producing, fabricating, processing, printing, or imprinting of tangible personal property for a consideration for consumers who furnish either directly or indirectly the materials used in the producing, fabricating, processing, printing, or imprinting. (c) The furnishing and distributing of tangible personal property for a consideration by social clubs and fraternal organizations to their members or others. (d) The furnishing, preparing, or serving for a consideration of food, meals, or drinks. (e) A transaction whereby the possession of property is transferred but the seller retains the title as security for
perturbation.justification: The changed text redefines 'distributor' to include entities acquiring products for internal use, and broadens the definition of 'end use customers.' This contradicts California Revenue and Taxation Code Section 6006, as it obscures the point of sale by including internal use as distribution. As stated in snippet 1, 6006. “Sale” means and includes: (a) Any transfer of title or possession... of tangible personal property for a consideration.” By blurring the lines between resale and internal use, the modification impacts sales tax collection and remittance, which is a key aspect of the law.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement may not be assigned by either party without prior written permission from the other party, which permission shall not be unreasonably withheld or delayed. Any attempt by either party to assign any right, or delegate any duty or obligation which arises under the Agreement without such permission will be voidable.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement may be assigned by either party to any subsidiary or affiliated company without prior written permission. All other assignments require only notification, not permission, which cannot be challenged. Any attempt to challenge will result in a penalty of 10% of the annual profits.
perturbation.explanation: Restricting the 'reasonableness' requirement for assignment and introducing penalty clauses is inconsistent with contract law principles governing assignment and restraint of trade. The law allows assignment, with the caveats of what is reasonable.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600
perturbation.law_explanation: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600 generally invalidates contracts that restrain a person from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business. While this pertains to employment usually, by adding in challenging will result in a penalty, then this is essentially restraining the party, which may not be in accordance to the law.

GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 1. Contracts in Restraint of Trade [16600 - 16607] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) 16600. (a) Except as provided in this chapter, every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void. (b) (1) This section shall be read broadly, in accordance with Edwards v. Arthur Andersen LLP (2008) 44 Cal.4th 937, to void the application of any noncompete agreement in an employment context, or any noncompete clause in an employment contract, no matter how narrowly tailored, that does not satisfy an exception in this chapter. (2) This subdivision does not constitute a change in, but is declaratory of, existing law. (c) This section shall not be limited to contracts where the person being restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business is a party to the contract. (Amended by Stats. 2023, Ch. 828, Sec. 1. (AB 1076) Effective January 1, 2024.)

Contracts In Restraint Of Trade BUSINESS AND PROFESSIONS CODE SECTION 16600-16607 16600. Except as provided in this chapter, every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void. 16601. Any person who sells the goodwill of a business, or any owner of a business entity selling or otherwise disposing of all of his or her ownership interest in the business entity, or any owner of a business entity that sells (a) all or substantially all of its operating assets together with the goodwill of the business entity, (b) all or substantially all of the operating assets of a division or a subsidiary of the business entity together with the goodwill of that division or subsidiary, or (c) all of the ownership interest of any subsidiary, may agree with the buyer to refrain from carrying on a similar business within a specified geographic area in which the business so sold, or that of the business entity, division, or subsidiary has been carried on, so long as the buyer, or any person deriving title to the goodwill or ownership interest from the buyer, carries on a like business therein. For the purposes of this section, "business entity" means any partnership (including a limited partnership or a limited liability partnership), limited liability company (including a series of a limited liability company formed under the laws of a jurisdiction that recognizes such a series), or corporation. For the purposes of this section, "owner of a business entity" means any partner, in the case of a business entity that is a partnership (including a limited partnership or a limited liability partnership), or any member, in the case of a business entity that is a limited liability company (including a series of a limited liability company formed under the laws of a jurisdiction that recognizes such a series), or any owner of capital stock, in the case of a business entity that is a corporation. For the purposes of this section, "ownership interest" means a partnership interest, in the
perturbation.justification: The modified contract text introduces a penalty for challenging assignment terms, potentially restraining a party from engaging in lawful business practices. This contradicts California Business and Professions Code Section 16600, which states that "every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void." The original contract allowed for reasonable challenges, while the modified version penalizes them, thus creating a restraint. The added penalty directly conflicts with the law's aim to prevent contracts that unduly restrict business activities.
